steps.title: Categorize the equation types in the choices
steps.content:
Scanning the choices:
  • A and B are linear equations ($y = mx + b$ form)
  • C and D are exponential equations ($y = ab^x$ form)
Look at the scatterplot pattern: do the data points follow a curve that accelerates upward (exponential), or increase at roughly constant increments (linear)? The vertical gaps between consecutive points appear relatively uniform—approximately 300–700 units of $y$ per roughly 1000-unit increment in $x$. This steady rate of increase indicates a linear relationship, not exponential.

steps.title: Eliminate choices with wrong slope direction
steps.content:
The scatterplot clearly shows $y$ increasing as $x$ increases. This requires a positive slope.

Choice B: $y = 694 - 1.2x$ has slope $-1.2$ (negative), meaning $y$ would decrease as $x$ increases. This contradicts the visible trend, so B is eliminated.

Choices C and D are exponential and were already eliminated. We're left with choice A.

steps.title: Verify choice A fits the data
steps.content:
Choice A: $y = -694 + 1.2x$

Check against a clear data point in the middle of the scatterplot. The point near $x = 3000$ appears to have $y \approx 2900$.
$$y = -694 + 1.2(3000) = -694 + 3600 = 2906$$
The predicted value $2906$ matches the observed point at approximately $2900$. This close fit confirms the linear model with slope $1.2$ and intercept $-694$ is appropriate. That matches choice A — A is correct.
